FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study (n=9,208), baseline ages 9-10 years old (2016-2018) and 2-year follow-up (2018-2020)

FT_IC2.evidence: Specific smartphone/screen modalities measured: viewing TV shows/movies, watching videos (YouTube), playing video games, texting, video chatting (Skype/FaceTime), social media (Facebook/Instagram/Twitter)

FT_IC3.evidence: Logistic regression analyses examining associations between baseline self-reported screen time (exposure) and OCD at 2-year follow-up (outcome). Multiple models tested: unadjusted, adjusted for confounders including baseline OCD, and excluding baseline OCD participants

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple odds ratios reported: total screen time aOR 1.05 (95% CI 1.01-1.09); video games aOR 1.15 (95% CI 1.03-1.28); watching videos aOR 1.11 (95% CI 1.01-1.23)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical prospective cohort study analyzing ABCD data with 2-year follow-up

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Journal of Adolescent Health (2023). Not a preprint or duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: FT_EC7 does not apply: (a) Screen time is independent variable with non-SM outcome (OCD diagnosis), not dependent variable; (b) Predictor includes individual psychological variable (OCD, mental health outcome), not demographic-only; (c) Study is prospective but direction irrelevant because screen time predicts mental health outcome, not vice versa
notes: Strong prospective evidence with multiple screen modality measures. Video games and videos showed strongest associations with new-onset OCD

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study, Year 2 data (2018-2020), n=10,280 early adolescents aged 10-14 (48.8% female)

FT_IC2.evidence: Multiple smartphone/screen behaviors measured: having TV/Internet-connected device in bedroom, phone ringer status, bedtime screen activities (streaming movies, video games, music, talking/texting, social media, chat rooms, Internet browsing, computer/laptop for studying, reading), phone notifications waking during night

FT_IC3.evidence: Modified Poisson regression analyses examining associations between bedtime screen use (exposure) and sleep outcomes (trouble falling/staying asleep, overall sleep disturbance). Adjusted for multiple confounders including depression, pandemic period

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple adjusted risk ratios reported: TV/device in bedroom ARR 1.27 (95% CI 1.12-1.44); leaving ringer on ARR 1.23 (95% CI 1.03-1.46); chat rooms ARR 1.27 (95% CI 1.17-1.39); video games ARR 1.22 (95% CI 1.14-1.29); social media ARR 1.16 (95% CI 1.10-1.23)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis from ABCD Study Year 2 data

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Sleep Health: Journal of the National Sleep Foundation (2023). Not a preprint or duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: FT_EC7 does not apply: (a) Study is cross-sectional (not longitudinal prospective as required by FT_EC7); (b) Screen time predicts non-SM outcome (sleep disturbance), not functioning as dependent variable with demographic predictors
notes: Cross-sectional study examining bedtime screen behaviors and sleep outcomes. Multiple screen modalities examined individually with extractable statistics. Comprehensive measure of contemporary bedtime device use

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study (N=11,875), baseline assessment (2016-2018), ages 9-11 years old; 1-year follow-up (2017-2019), ages 10-11 years old

FT_IC2.evidence: Multiple specific screen modalities measured: viewing/streaming TV shows or movies, watching/streaming videos, playing video games, texting, video chatting (e.g., Skype, FaceTime), and social media (e.g., Facebook, Instagram, Twitter)

FT_IC3.evidence: Modified Poisson regression analyses examining prospective associations between baseline screen time (exposure variable) and conduct disorder or oppositional defiant disorder at 1-year follow-up (outcome variables), adjusting for confounders

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple prevalence ratios reported: social media PR 1.62 (95% CI 1.39-1.87) for conduct disorder; video chat PR 1.21 (95% CI 1.06-1.37); texting PR 1.19 (95% CI 1.07-1.33); television/movies PR 1.17 (95% CI 1.10-1.25); video games PR 1.14 (95% CI 1.07-1.21) for oppositional defiant disorder

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical prospective cohort study analyzing baseline and 1-year follow-up ABCD data

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Journal of Child Psychology and Psychiatry (2023). Not a preprint or duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: FT_EC7 does not apply: (a) Screen time is independent variable with non-SM outcome (conduct/oppositional defiant disorder), not dependent variable; (b) Predictors include individual behavioral/psychological outcome variables, not demographic-only; (c) Prospective design with screen time predicting behavioral disorder outcomes
notes: Strong prospective evidence with multiple screen modality measures. Social media showed strongest association with conduct disorder (62% higher prevalence). Study examined thresholds showing >4 hours total screen time associated with 69% higher conduct disorder prevalence and 46% higher oppositional defiant disorder prevalence
